title: Mulligatawny
subtitle: Anglo-Indian spiced lentil and chicken soup
excerpt: A thick, golden soup of red lentils, chicken stock, and mild curry spices, finished with a swirl of cream and a squeeze of lemon. Mulligatawny is an Anglo-Indian dish from the Raj period, based on a Tamil pepper water (milagu tannir) and elaborated by the British in India into a substantial, warming soup. Still served in old-fashioned British restaurants and at home as a starter.
sourceNotes: Mulligatawny derives from the Tamil 'milagu-tannir' (pepper water), a thin, spiced broth that the British in India elaborated into a substantial soup by adding meat, lentils, apple, and cream. Mrs Beeton's Household Management (1861) includes a version. It became a staple of Anglo-Indian domestic cooking and British hotel menus.

content.text: Mulligatawny gets its characteristic sweet-savoury balance from the apple, which cooks down into the soup and adds a gentle fruitiness alongside the spices. Don't omit it. It is the element that makes mulligatawny recognisably itself rather than simply a mild chicken and lentil soup. The apple should be completely dissolved into the blended base; no apple pieces should remain in the finished soup.

The spicing is mild, this is a starter soup with warming spices rather than curry heat. The curry powder gives colour and warmth; the fresh ginger adds brightness. Blending the soup before adding the cream gives a smooth base; the cream is stirred in at the end and should not be boiled after it is added.

Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and {{carrot}} and cook for 8 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{curry-powder}}, and {{turmeric}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{apple}}, {{red-lentils}}, and {{chicken-stock}}. Bring to the boil, then reduce to a simmer and cook for 20 minutes until the lentils are completely soft. Blend until smooth. Return to the pan over low heat and stir in the {{double-cream}} and lemon juice. Season with salt. Serve with a swirl of extra cream.

items.fix: Add stock or water after blending until the desired consistency is reached. Mulligatawny should be creamy-thick, not gluey
items.cause: Lentils absorbed more liquid than expected

items.fix: Ensure lentils are fully soft before blending. If too thin after blending, simmer uncovered to reduce
items.cause: Too much stock or lentils not fully cooked

items.fix: The lemon juice is the final balancing element. Add it cautiously at the end and taste. It should brighten without making the soup sour
items.cause: Lemon juice omitted or insufficient seasoning

items.fix: Remove from heat or reduce to the lowest setting before stirring in the cream. Do not boil after adding
items.cause: Soup too hot when cream added

content.text: Mulligatawny is one of the oldest Anglo-Indian dishes still in use. It is a Victorian-era soup that lasted because the taste is good. The apple-and-spice mix gives it a character other creamed soups don't have. It suits home cooking. The ingredients are pantry staples and the technique is simple blending. Its link to Indian cooking is historical, not stylistic. The flavour is closer to an English cream soup than a curry-house dish.
